svg.title: VoroGaze AOI: From facial landmarks to defensible areas of interest
svg.desc: A0 portrait research-methods poster by Matthew J. Green, Psychology, Bournemouth University.
svg.text: From facial landmarks to defensible areas of interest
Inspectable inputs · deterministic boundaries · traceable outputs

text.tspan: A neat summary can conceal a broken mapping.
Before counting fixations, researchers must know
that screen, stimulus and gaze occupy one coherent
VoroGaze makes the screen, face placement and
fixations visible together – so mismatches can be
caught before AOI metrics are computed.
One eye-tracking expert and one non-specialist
completed core upload/download tasks. The expert
described the tool as useful and self-explanatory.
The non-specialist found AOI assignment after
The bundled Lisa1 fixture keeps every step visible.

svg.text: The full audit trail precedes the summary
Worked fixture output – illustrative, not an empirical finding.
Map columns; view screen, face and fixations.
Click, label and edit landmarks on the face.
Nearest centre = the point’s Voronoi cell.
Every fixation retains its source row and
Validity still depends on defensible landmark
PROTOTYPE – METHODS WORKFLOW, NOT EMPIRICAL VALIDATION
VoroGaze turns AOI construction into an inspectable analysis chain –
make the geometry visible, make the boundaries reproducible, then compute the metrics.
Face: DeBruine, L. & Jones, B. (2017), Face Research Lab London Set, CC BY 4.0 · doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.5047666
Prototype: github.com/mjgreen/vorogazeaoi · Public method demonstration uses the bundled Lisa1 fixture only.
Areas of interest you can see – and defend.
